pageCaption.value: Inza Koné, Côte d’Ivoire’s first primatologist, has spent almost 20 years working in Tanoé-Ehy, one of West Africa’s last primary rainforests.

WaPageTitle.value: 2023 Rolex Awards for Enterprise Laureate - Inza Koné

metadescription.value: Primatologist Inza Koné is leading community-based conservation efforts to protect the biodiversity of Côte d'Ivoire's Tanoé-Ehy forest. More on rolex.org.

text.value:
A part of the Perpetual Planet Initiative, the Rolex Awards for Enterprise support exceptional individuals who have the courage and conviction to take on major challenges, initiating extraordinary projects that make the world a better place.

Protecting Africa’s forests

Inza Koné, Côte d’Ivoire’s first primatologist, has spent almost 20 years working in Tanoé-Ehy, one of West Africa’s last primary rainforests. The vast swampy forest, tucked away in the furthest south-eastern corner of his country, is home to animals and plants found nowhere else in the world. Koné has been researching its critically endangered species and creating a model for conservation centred on communities that live there.

title.value:
Inza Koné: Episode 3
Back to the roots

pageTitle.value: 2023 Rolex Awards Laureate - Inza Koné - Episode 3

pageCaption.value: A 2023 Rolex Awards Laureate, Inza Koné is on a mission to help people and nature successfully coexist and protect one of Côte d’Ivoire’s last remaining ancient rainforests, Tanoé-Ehy.

WaPageTitle.value: 2023 Rolex Awards Laureate - Inza Koné - Episode 3

metadescription.value: Rolex Awards Laureate Inza Koné is involving local communities in his efforts to preserve Côte d'Ivoire's Tanoé-Ehy forest. Watch the video on rolex.org.

previewTitle.value: 2023 Rolex Awards Laureate: Inza Koné - Episode 3

text.value: Tanoé-Ehy contains at least 45 endemic species of plants and animals. Koné is particularly interested in the forest’s primates: “In these 11,000 hectares of swampy forest, we have some of the most endangered species of monkey in West Africa.” This includes the possibly extinct Miss Waldron’s red colobus, which has not been scientifically documented since the 1970s. However, locals continue to report sightings within the forest and Koné himself has heard its distinctive cries, hence the search continues.

Despite Côte d’Ivoire having the highest level of biodiversity in West Africa, human activity has left only two per cent of its primary forests intact. In 2006, this led Koné to establish a conservation programme involving 11 communities living in Tanoé-Ehy. They successfully protected it from an oil palm development plan and, in 2021, the forest was officially declared a community-managed nature reserve. It is now collectively managed and owned by the communities. For Koné, the success of the campaign comes down to their involvement: “Eleven villages have joined forces to preserve their common heritage.”
Eleven villages have joined forces to preserve their common heritage.

The dense vegetation and swampy conditions of Tanoé-Ehy have helped to protect it from significant hunting and farming, but also make it harder to explore and study. However, with the expertise of locals who once were poachers, the team is able to carry out monthly expeditions into the forest. “These former hunters have knowledge that the most advanced scientists don’t have,” Koné says.

He provides locals with technical support and training in environmental monitoring techniques such as drone surveys, eDNA sampling and camera trap monitoring. He hopes the communities will eventually be able to manage the forest autonomously. To encourage this, the team works with the 11 communities to find alternative, sustainable livelihoods such as cassava farming. Thanks to the provision of modern equipment in a small processing plant, the income of cassava farmers has already improved fivefold. The team has also created an educational botanical garden in each village, planted natural fences separating farmland from forest and planted more than 10,000 native trees to reforest the land around the villages.

Koné hopes to expand the protection of the Tanoé-Ehy forest across the border into neighbouring Ghana. By working with authorities and communities in both countries, he wants to create a sustainable future for local communities in one of the world’s most biodiverse forests.

pageTitle.value: 2023 Rolex Awards for Enterprise Laureate - Constantino Aucca Chutas

pageCaption.value: As the descendent of Quechuan farmers, Peruvian biologist Constantino Aucca Chutas felt a responsibility to act when he saw how the forests of his ancestral Andean home were disappearing because of human activity.

WaPageTitle.value: 2023 Rolex Awards for Enterprise Laureate - Constantino Aucca Chutas

metadescription.value: Biologist Constantino Aucca Chutas is leading reforestation efforts in the Andes by involving local and indigenous communities. More on rolex.org.

pageTitle.value: 2023 Rolex Awards for Enterprise Laureate - Denica Riadini-Flesch

pageCaption.value: Social entrepreneur Denica Riadini-Flesch has succeeded in creating one of the world’s first regenerative farm-to-closet clothing chains – SukkhaCitta, meaning happiness. Determined to empower rural artisans in Indonesia, she has created hundreds of jobs with fair pay for those who craft high-end clothing using traditional and sustainable techniques.

WaPageTitle.value: 2023 Rolex Awards for Enterprise Laureate - Denica Riadini-Flesch
